claims.claim_text: The module says the most common DevSecOps failure pattern starts with a tool-first investment.
claims.span_hint: line 54 | section: Culture Is the Operating System for Security Work
claims.anchor_text: The most common DevSecOps failure pattern begins with a tool-first investment.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A common DevSecOps failure pattern begins with a tool-first investment.
claims.rationale: This is a prevalence claim without a cited survey or dataset, so the unsupported superlative should be removed.

claims.claim_text: Effective security champions programs need management buy-in and protected time rather than title-only designation.
claims.span_hint: line 776 | section: Did You Know? | item 1
claims.rationale: OWASP's Security Champions guidance directly says champions need management buy-in and a percentage of time set aside from regular work.

claims.claim_text: Blameless postmortem culture in tech drew on safety-critical fields such as healthcare and avionics.
claims.span_hint: line 780 | section: Did You Know? | item 3
claims.rationale: Google's SRE postmortem-culture chapter directly states this origin story, but `sre.google` is outside the current allowlist.

further_reading.title: OWASP Security Champions
further_reading.why_relevant: Direct guidance on champion scope, time allocation, management buy-in, and program design.

further_reading.title: NIST Secure Software Development Framework (SSDF)
further_reading.why_relevant: Provides an authoritative framework for organizational preparation, vulnerability response, and secure-development governance.

notes: Skipped code fences, YAML examples, quiz items, hands-on exercise steps, and hypothetical scorecard/example numbers as pedagogical or non-inline-citation material per the prompt. The only off-allowlist claim retained is the safety-critical origin of blameless postmortems.
